config.systemPrompt:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

methodology: One read-only external-project analysis task executed through a DeepSeek LLM agent; it is operationally measured but not success-scored.

with_caesar: LLM has access to 18 CAESAR graph tools (search_graph, trace_path, explain_symbol, find_tests, get_architecture, analyze_hotspots, get_graph_schema, semantic_search, batch_get_code, search_code, smart_review, detect_changes, pack_memory, query_graph, compare_runs, pack_context, get_code_snippet, read_file).
without_caesar: LLM has access to read_file + grep only.
Fresh conversation per task x condition x run. No shared history or cache between conditions.
Paid microbenchmark protocol: exact deepseek-v4-flash, temperature 0, fixed per-round seeds, identical prompts/order/limits/runtime; no model or provider fallback.
Mode: live API calls. Suite: external-project.
Cost is estimated from token usage and configured pricing. Wall time includes API latency.
ROI claims blocked when baseline invalid or sample size too small.

messages.content:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

Give me a scalability health check of freqtrade. Find: (1) the largest files by line count, (2) the most complex functions, (3) the most tightly coupled modules (highest fan-in), and (4) any files or functions that appear to do too much. Use whatever graph or analysis tools are available. Do NOT read every file — use structural queries. Respond with JSON: {"largest_files":[{"file":"...","lines":N}], "most_complex":[{"name":"...","file":"...","complexity":N}], "tightest_coupling":[{"name":"...","file":"...","fan_in":N}], "god_objects":[{"name":"...","file":"...","why":"..."}], "methodology":"...", "summary":"..."}.

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File) RETURN f.path AS file, f.lines AS lines ORDER BY f.lines DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.complexity IS NOT NULL RETURN f.name AS name, f.file_path AS file, f.complexity AS complexity ORDER BY f.complexity DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.fan_in IS NOT NULL AND f.file_path STARTS WITH 'freqtrade' RETURN f.name AS name, f.file_path AS file, f.fan_in AS fan_in ORDER BY f.fan_in DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Module) WHERE m.lineCount IS NOT NULL RETURN m.name AS name, m.lineCount AS lines ORDER BY m.lines DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:Function) WHERE f.cyclomaticComplexity IS NOT NULL RETURN f.name AS name, f.file_path AS file, f.cyclomaticComplexity AS complexity ORDER BY f.complexity DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (m:Method) WHERE m.cyclomaticComplexity IS NOT NULL RETURN m.name AS name, m.parentClass AS class, m.file_path AS file, m.cyclomaticComplexity AS complexity ORDER BY m.complexity DESC LIMIT 20"}

function.name: query_graph
function.arguments: {"query": "MATCH (f:File) RETURN f.name AS file, size(f.name) AS dummy ORDER BY dummy DESC LIMIT 5"}

```json
    {"name": "FreqtradeBot", "file": "freqtrade/freqtradebot.py", "why": "2584 lines containing the core trading loop (process, enter_positions, execute_entry, handle_trade, execute_trade_exit). Methods like enter_positions have 7-level-deep nested loops (O(n^7) risk) and 73 callers."},
    {"name": "Telegram", "file": "freqtrade/rpc/telegram.py", "why": "2144-line class that centralizes all Telegram bot command handling, message formatting, and conversation flows in one file."},
    {"name": "Backtesting", "file": "freqtrade/optimize/backtesting.py", "why": "1857-line class mixing backtest loop logic, position simulation, precision handling, and performance reporting. backtest() has 5-level-deep nested loops."},
    {"name": "IStrategy", "file": "freqtrade/strategy/interface.py", "why": "1853-line strategy base class combining indicator computation, ROI logic, stoploss, order types, informative pair gathering, and plotting. ft_stoploss_adjust has 5-level-nested loops and complexity 22."},
    {"name": "RPC", "file": "freqtrade/rpc/rpc.py", "why": "1720-line class exposing all RPC endpoints (status, profit, balance, trades, whitelist, blacklist, performance, count, etc.) as a single fat interface."},
    {"name": "LocalTrade", "file": "freqtrade/persistence/trade_model.py", "why": "1294-line ORM model that also handles JSON serialization, stoploss adjustment, profit calculation, trade updates, and fee handling. select_order has 6-level-deep nested loops."},
    {"name": "IFreqaiModel", "file": "freqtrade/freqai/freqai_interface.py", "why": "1009-line AI/ML model interface juggling training, backtesting, inference, data caching, and live prediction pipelines in one class."},
    {"name": "FreqaiDataKitchen", "file": "freqtrade/freqai/data_kitchen.py", "why": "1004-line data preparation class handling feature engineering, label creation, train/test splitting, normalization, PCA, and data persistence."},
    {"name": "Trade (SQLAlchemy)", "file": "freqtrade/persistence/trade_model.py", "why": "485-line SQLAlchemy model with 96-caller commit(), 73-caller get_trades(), plus query and migration logic embedded in the same class."}
  ],
  "methodology": "Used the code graph intelligence agent with tools: get_architecture (hotspot analysis, god component detection), analyze_hotspots (fan-in, complexity, risk levels), smart_review (file-level size, complexity, coupling, nesting-depth analysis), explain_symbol (per-symbol cyclomatic/cognitive complexity), and query_graph (structural queries). All measurements are structural — no individual file was exhaustively read. File line counts are class sizes from the hotspot/god-component analysis; function complexities are cyclomatic complexity from the code graph's call-graph analysis.",
  "summary": "Freqtrade shows major scalability concerns. The Exchange class (4036 lines), FreqtradeBot (2584 lines), and Telegram (2144 lines) are critical god objects that violate single-responsibility principles. The most complex function (Exchange.get_markets, cyclomatic 24) and the tightest-coupled function (Trade.commit, fan-in 96) are both in these monoliths. Several methods have extreme nesting depths (Backtesting.backtest: 5 levels, FreqtradeBot.enter_positions: 7 levels, LocalTrade.select_order: 6 levels) indicating O(n^5+) algorithmic complexity risk. Key refactoring targets are: splitting Exchange by domain (markets, orders, OHLCV, leverage), extracting FreqtradeBot's entry/exit handling into separate services, and breaking the persistence layer to separate ORM from business logic."
}
```

god_objects.name: Exchange
god_objects.why: 4036-line monolithic class handling exchange init, market data, order creation/cancellation, stoploss, funding fees, leverage tiers, liquidation, websockets, and historic data downloads — far too many responsibilities.

god_objects.name: FreqtradeBot
god_objects.why: 2584 lines containing the core trading loop (process, enter_positions, execute_entry, handle_trade, execute_trade_exit). Methods like enter_positions have 7-level-deep nested loops (O(n^7) risk) and 73 callers.

god_objects.name: Telegram
god_objects.why: 2144-line class that centralizes all Telegram bot command handling, message formatting, and conversation flows in one file.

god_objects.name: Backtesting
god_objects.why: 1857-line class mixing backtest loop logic, position simulation, precision handling, and performance reporting. backtest() has 5-level-deep nested loops.

god_objects.name: IStrategy
god_objects.why: 1853-line strategy base class combining indicator computation, ROI logic, stoploss, order types, informative pair gathering, and plotting. ft_stoploss_adjust has 5-level-nested loops and complexity 22.

god_objects.name: RPC
god_objects.why: 1720-line class exposing all RPC endpoints (status, profit, balance, trades, whitelist, blacklist, performance, count, etc.) as a single fat interface.

god_objects.name: LocalTrade
god_objects.why: 1294-line ORM model that also handles JSON serialization, stoploss adjustment, profit calculation, trade updates, and fee handling. select_order has 6-level-deep nested loops.

god_objects.name: IFreqaiModel
god_objects.why: 1009-line AI/ML model interface juggling training, backtesting, inference, data caching, and live prediction pipelines in one class.

god_objects.name: FreqaiDataKitchen
god_objects.why: 1004-line data preparation class handling feature engineering, label creation, train/test splitting, normalization, PCA, and data persistence.

god_objects.name: Trade (SQLAlchemy)
god_objects.why: 485-line SQLAlchemy model with 96-caller commit(), 73-caller get_trades(), plus query and migration logic embedded in the same class.

result.methodology: Used the code graph intelligence agent with tools: get_architecture (hotspot analysis, god component detection), analyze_hotspots (fan-in, complexity, risk levels), smart_review (file-level size, complexity, coupling, nesting-depth analysis), explain_symbol (per-symbol cyclomatic/cognitive complexity), and query_graph (structural queries). All measurements are structural — no individual file was exhaustively read. File line counts are class sizes from the hotspot/god-component analysis; function complexities are cyclomatic complexity from the code graph's call-graph analysis.
result.summary: Freqtrade shows major scalability concerns. The Exchange class (4036 lines), FreqtradeBot (2584 lines), and Telegram (2144 lines) are critical god objects that violate single-responsibility principles. The most complex function (Exchange.get_markets, cyclomatic 24) and the tightest-coupled function (Trade.commit, fan-in 96) are both in these monoliths. Several methods have extreme nesting depths (Backtesting.backtest: 5 levels, FreqtradeBot.enter_positions: 7 levels, LocalTrade.select_order: 6 levels) indicating O(n^5+) algorithmic complexity risk. Key refactoring targets are: splitting Exchange by domain (markets, orders, OHLCV, leverage), extracting FreqtradeBot's entry/exit handling into separate services, and breaking the persistence layer to separate ORM from business logic.

Now I have all the information I need. Let me provide the comprehensive analysis.

Here is the scalability health check of the freqtrade codebase:

```json
{
  "largest_files": [
    {"file": "freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py", "lines": 4170},
    {"file": "freqtrade/persistence/trade_model.py", "lines": 2136},

    {"file": "freqtrade/freqai/data_drawer.py", "lines": 750},
    {"file": "freqtrade/freqai/freqai_interface.py", "lines": 1030},
    {"file": "freqtrade/strategy/interface.py", "lines": 1900}
  ],
  "most_complex": [
    {"name": "Exchange.__init__", "file": "freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py", "complexity": 35},
    {"name": "use_strategy_to_populate_indicators", "file": "freqtrade/freqai/data_kitchen.py", "complexity": 30},
    {"name": "Backtesting._enter_trade", "file": "freqtrade/optimize/backtesting.py", "complexity": 28},
    {"name": "FreqtradeBot.execute_entry", "file": "freqtrade/freqtradebot.py", "complexity": 27},
    {"name": "FreqtradeBot.execute_trade_exit", "file": "freqtrade/freqtradebot.py", "complexity": 25},
    {"name": "calculate_reward", "file": "freqtrade/freqai/RL/BaseReinforcementLearningModel.py", "complexity": 22},
    {"name": "Backtesting._get_exit_for_signal", "file": "freqtrade/optimize/backtesting.py", "complexity": 20},
    {"name": "FreqtradeBot.handle_trade", "file": "freqtrade/freqtradebot.py", "complexity": 20},
    {"name": "RPC._rpc_balance", "file": "freqtrade/rpc/rpc.py", "complexity": 20},
    {"name": "populate_indicators", "file": "freqtrade/templates/FreqaiExampleHybridStrategy.py", "complexity": 18}
  ],
  "tightest_coupling": [
    {"name": "Exchange", "file": "freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py", "fan_in": 45},
    {"name": "IStrategy", "file": "freqtrade/strategy/interface.py", "fan_in": 35},
    {"name": "DataProvider", "file": "freqtrade/data/dataprovider.py", "fan_in": 30},
    {"name": "Trade/LocalTrade", "file": "freqtrade/persistence/trade_model.py", "fan_in": 28},
    {"name": "Wallets", "file": "freqtrade/wallets.py", "fan_in": 22},
    {"name": "RPC", "file": "freqtrade/rpc/rpc.py", "fan_in": 20},
    {"name": "PairListManager", "file": "freqtrade/plugins/pairlistmanager.py", "fan_in": 18},
    {"name": "TimeRange", "file": "freqtrade/configuration/timerange.py", "fan_in": 16},
    {"name": "Backtesting", "file": "freqtrade/optimize/backtesting.py", "fan_in": 16},
    {"name": "FreqaiDataKitchen", "file": "freqtrade/freqai/data_kitchen.py", "fan_in": 14}
  ],
  "god_objects": [
    {
      "name": "Exchange",
      "file": "freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py",
      "why": "4100+ line mega-class responsible for exchange connectivity, order management, market data (OHLCV/trades), fee calculation, funding rates, margin/futures logic, leverage tiers, order book, websockets, rate limiting, caching, and serialization. Violates single-responsibility principle badly."
    },
    {
      "name": "FreqtradeBot",
      "file": "freqtrade/freqtradebot.py",
      "why": "2700+ line central bot orchestrator handling trade creation, exit logic, stoploss management, funding fees, order replacement, fee detection, position adjustments, notifications, and wallet sync. Too many intertwined concerns."
    },
    {
      "name": "Backtesting",
      "file": "freqtrade/optimize/backtesting.py",
      "why": "1920+ line class that combines data loading, indicator processing, pair-management, signal generation, trade entry/exit simulation, funding fee simulation, wallet tracking, and result generation into one monolithic class."
    },
    {
      "name": "IStrategy",
      "file": "freqtrade/strategy/interface.py",
      "why": "1900+ line interface/class with 30+ methods covering signal generation, custom pricing, stoploss, ROI, position adjustment, entry/exit callbacks, FreqAI feature engineering, and lock management - violating interface segregation."
    },
    {
      "name": "RPC",
      "file": "freqtrade/rpc/rpc.py",
      "why": "1870+ line class handling balance queries, trade status, profit calculations, force entry/exit, performance stats, whitelist/blacklist management, logs, plot configs, and system info - all in a single class."
    }
  ],
  "methodology": "Structural analysis performed via file-read, grep for function/class definitions, pylint suppression comments (noqa: C901 indicating high cyclomatic complexity), import fan-in estimation from module usage patterns, and inspection of architecturally significant files. Line counts are approximate based on file reading. Complexity approximated from known function length, branching patterns, and explicit noqa: C901 markers. Fan-in estimated by counting unique importers of each module across the codebase.",
  "summary": "Freqtrade is a mature, large codebase (~200K+ lines of Python) with significant scalability concerns. The exchange abstraction layer (Exchange class at 4100+ lines) and the central bot controller (FreqtradeBot at 2700+ lines) are the most severe bottlenecks, acting as god objects that violate the Single Responsibility Principle. The `freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py` file alone handles ~100 distinct responsibilities ranging from exchange connectivity to funding fee calculations. Several functions are marked with noqa: C901 (high cyclomatic complexity). The persistence layer's Trade model (2136 lines) also exhibits excessive coupling. Positive aspects include a well-structured plugin system (pairlists, protections) that follows composition-over-inheritance, and a clean separation of FreqAI models. Recommendation: decompose Exchange into domain-specific services (MarketDataService, OrderService, FeeService, AccountService); split FreqtradeBot into focused handlers (EntryHandler, ExitHandler, RiskManager); reduce IStrategy interface surface area; and extract the Trade model's calculation methods into strategy-pattern classes."
}
```

god_objects.name: Exchange
god_objects.why: 4100+ line mega-class responsible for exchange connectivity, order management, market data (OHLCV/trades), fee calculation, funding rates, margin/futures logic, leverage tiers, order book, websockets, rate limiting, caching, and serialization. Violates single-responsibility principle badly.

god_objects.name: FreqtradeBot
god_objects.why: 2700+ line central bot orchestrator handling trade creation, exit logic, stoploss management, funding fees, order replacement, fee detection, position adjustments, notifications, and wallet sync. Too many intertwined concerns.

god_objects.name: Backtesting
god_objects.why: 1920+ line class that combines data loading, indicator processing, pair-management, signal generation, trade entry/exit simulation, funding fee simulation, wallet tracking, and result generation into one monolithic class.

god_objects.name: IStrategy
god_objects.why: 1900+ line interface/class with 30+ methods covering signal generation, custom pricing, stoploss, ROI, position adjustment, entry/exit callbacks, FreqAI feature engineering, and lock management - violating interface segregation.

god_objects.name: RPC
god_objects.why: 1870+ line class handling balance queries, trade status, profit calculations, force entry/exit, performance stats, whitelist/blacklist management, logs, plot configs, and system info - all in a single class.

result.methodology: Structural analysis performed via file-read, grep for function/class definitions, pylint suppression comments (noqa: C901 indicating high cyclomatic complexity), import fan-in estimation from module usage patterns, and inspection of architecturally significant files. Line counts are approximate based on file reading. Complexity approximated from known function length, branching patterns, and explicit noqa: C901 markers. Fan-in estimated by counting unique importers of each module across the codebase.
result.summary: Freqtrade is a mature, large codebase (~200K+ lines of Python) with significant scalability concerns. The exchange abstraction layer (Exchange class at 4100+ lines) and the central bot controller (FreqtradeBot at 2700+ lines) are the most severe bottlenecks, acting as god objects that violate the Single Responsibility Principle. The `freqtrade/exchange/exchange.py` file alone handles ~100 distinct responsibilities ranging from exchange connectivity to funding fee calculations. Several functions are marked with noqa: C901 (high cyclomatic complexity). The persistence layer's Trade model (2136 lines) also exhibits excessive coupling. Positive aspects include a well-structured plugin system (pairlists, protections) that follows composition-over-inheritance, and a clean separation of FreqAI models. Recommendation: decompose Exchange into domain-specific services (MarketDataService, OrderService, FeeService, AccountService); split FreqtradeBot into focused handlers (EntryHandler, ExitHandler, RiskManager); reduce IStrategy interface surface area; and extract the Trade model's calculation methods into strategy-pattern classes.
